title: PM Mudra Yojana
meta_description: PM Mudra Yojana 2026 — get collateral-free business loans up to ₹20 lakh. Shishu, Kishore, Tarun categories explained, eligibility, how to apply at any bank.

hero.one_liner: Collateral-free loans from ₹50,000 to ₹20 lakh for small businesses, street vendors, artisans, and micro-entrepreneurs — no guarantor needed

what_is_it: Pradhan Mantri Mudra Yojana (PMMY) was launched on 8 April 2015 to provide easy access to business loans for small and micro-enterprises that cannot get regular bank loans due to lack of collateral, credit history, or formal documentation. The scheme provides loans from ₹50,000 to ₹20 lakh through all commercial banks, RRBs, small finance banks, MFIs, and NBFCs. As of March 2026, the government expanded the maximum loan limit from ₹10 lakh to ₹20 lakh (Tarun Plus category) to support more ambitious business ventures.

MUDRA stands for Micro Units Development and Refinance Agency Ltd. — it is a refinancing agency under SIDBI. MUDRA itself doesn't lend to borrowers directly. Instead, it provides refinancing support to banks and lending institutions so they can lend to small businesses at affordable rates. This model ensures that the actual lending happens through traditional banking channels you already know and trust.

The scheme has three main categories based on loan amount: Shishu (up to ₹50,000) for those just starting their business, Kishore (₹50,001 to ₹5 lakh) for those looking to expand, and Tarun (₹5,00,001 to ₹20 lakh) for established small businesses needing more capital. No collateral or guarantor is required for any category. A new Tarun Plus category was introduced in October 2024, allowing businesses to borrow up to ₹20 lakh.

Since launch, PMMY has disbursed over ₹32.4 lakh crore across 52+ crore loan accounts, making it one of the world's largest micro-lending programs. About 68% of Mudra loans have gone to women entrepreneurs, and over 50% to SC/ST/OBC borrowers. The average loan size has grown from ₹38,000 in FY16 to over ₹1.02 lakh in FY25, reflecting how entrepreneurs are scaling their businesses.

eligibility.label: Who can apply
eligibility.value: Any Indian citizen with a business plan or existing small business — includes manufacturing, trading, services, and allied activities

eligibility.label: Business types covered
eligibility.value: Street vendors, shopkeepers, artisans, food stall owners, repair services, transport operators, tailors, salons, farmers for allied activities (dairy, poultry, beekeeping), and any micro-enterprise

eligibility.label: Collateral needed
eligibility.value: NO collateral or guarantor required for any Mudra loan category

eligibility.label: Shishu (up to ₹50K)
eligibility.value: For new businesses or those needing small working capital. Interest rate: 10-12% per annum

eligibility.label: Kishore (₹50K–₹5L)
eligibility.value: For businesses looking to expand. Need to show some business track record. Interest rate: 11-14% per annum

eligibility.label: Tarun (₹5L–₹20L)
eligibility.value: For established businesses needing larger capital. Bank may ask for business plan and financials. Interest rate: 12-16% per annum. Introduced as Tarun Plus in Oct 2024.

eligibility.label: Documents needed
eligibility.value: Aadhaar card, PAN card (for loans above ₹2 lakh), address proof, business proof (if existing), 2 passport photos, bank account statement (last 6 months for Kishore/Tarun)

extra_sections.heading: Shishu vs Kishore vs Tarun vs Tarun Plus — Which Should You Apply For?
rows: New businesses, street vendors, small working capital
Expanding businesses, buying equipment, increasing stock

extra_sections.heading: How to Improve Your Chances of Approval
content: Tip 1 — Start with Shishu, then upgrade: If you're a first-time borrower with no credit history, apply for a Shishu loan (up to ₹50,000) first. Repay it on time for 6-12 months, then apply for Kishore.
Banks are much more likely to approve larger loans for borrowers with a good repayment track record.

Tip 2 — Have a clear business plan: Even for Shishu loans, briefly explain what your business does, how you'll use the money, and how you'll repay. For Kishore and Tarun, prepare a simple one-page business plan with expected income and expenses.
You don't need a fancy document — even a handwritten plan works if it's clear and realistic.
Tip 3 — Apply at multiple banks: If one bank rejects your application, try another. Each bank has different risk criteria.
Nationalized banks (SBI, PNB, BOB) have mandated Mudra targets and are more likely to approve. Small finance banks (AU, Equitas, Ujjivan) are also very active in Mudra lending.
Tip 4 — Maintain a clean CIBIL score: For Kishore and Tarun loans, banks will check your credit score. If you have existing loans with missed payments, clear those first.
A CIBIL score of 650+ significantly improves your chances.

Tip 5 — Women and SC/ST applicants get priority: The government has mandated that at least 50% of Mudra loans go to women, SC/ST, and OBC borrowers. If you fall in these categories, explicitly mention it in your application.
Some banks have dedicated Mudra desks for women entrepreneurs.
Tip 6 — Don't approach agents or middlemen: Apply directly at the bank branch. Mudra loan processing is free — there is no service charge or processing fee for Shishu category.
If anyone asks you to pay money to 'arrange' a Mudra loan, it's a scam.

extra_sections.heading: What Mudra Loan Can Be Used For
content: Mudra loans can be used for virtually any legitimate business activity. Common uses include:
Working capital: Buying raw materials, inventory, or stock for your shop/business. This is the most common use — shopkeepers use it to stock goods before festival seasons.
Equipment purchase: Buying machinery, tools, sewing machines, food processing equipment, salon equipment, computers, or any business-related equipment.
Business expansion: Opening a second shop or outlet, renovating your existing workspace, adding new products or services to your business.
Vehicle purchase for business: Auto-rickshaws, e-rickshaws, delivery vehicles, tempo for goods transport. The vehicle must be used for commercial/business purposes.
Service businesses: Setting up a salon, tailoring unit, repair shop, coaching center, food stall, laundry service, or any other service-based micro-enterprise.

What you CANNOT use Mudra loan for: Personal expenses, home purchase or renovation, education fees, medical treatment, or any non-business purpose. If the bank discovers misuse, they can recall the full loan amount immediately.

how_to_apply.title: Visit any bank branch near you
how_to_apply.description: Go to any commercial bank (SBI, PNB, BOB, HDFC, ICICI, etc.), regional rural bank, or small finance bank. Ask for the Mudra loan application form. You can also apply online through some banks' websites — SBI eMudra and PNB Mudra portal offer direct online applications.

how_to_apply.title: Choose your category and fill the form
how_to_apply.description: Select Shishu, Kishore, Tarun, or Tarun Plus based on how much you need. Fill in your personal details, business details, loan amount, and repayment preference. Attach all required documents.

how_to_apply.title: Submit business plan (for Kishore/Tarun/Tarun Plus)
how_to_apply.description: For loans above ₹50,000, banks may ask for a brief business plan or project report. Describe: what your business does, how long you've been operating, monthly income/expenses, how the loan will help grow the business, and how you'll repay. Keep it simple and realistic.

how_to_apply.title: Bank processes and disburses
how_to_apply.description: The bank verifies your documents, checks your credit score (for Kishore/Tarun), and assesses your business viability. Shishu loans are processed in 3-7 days. Kishore takes 7-14 days. Tarun may take 2-4 weeks. Tarun Plus may take 4-6 weeks. Once approved, money is disbursed directly to your bank account.

important_alert.text: Mudra loan processing is FREE — there is no processing fee, service charge, or file charge for any category. Do NOT pay any middleman or agent to 'arrange' a Mudra loan. Apply directly at the bank. Complaints: 1800-11-0001 or visit the MUDRA official portal

important_dates.label: Application
important_dates.value: Open throughout the year at all banks

important_dates.label: No deadline
important_dates.value: Mudra is an ongoing scheme — apply anytime

important_dates.label: Mudra Card
important_dates.value: Available for working capital — works like a credit card for business expenses

faqs.question: Is Mudra loan really without collateral?
faqs.answer: Yes. No collateral, no guarantor, and no security deposit is required for any Mudra category (Shishu, Kishore, Tarun, or Tarun Plus up to ₹20 lakh). This is mandated by the government. If a bank asks for collateral for a Mudra loan, you can complain to the bank's grievance cell or call the Mudra helpline.

faqs.question: What is the interest rate on Mudra loans?
faqs.answer: Interest rates are not fixed by the government — each bank sets its own rate. Typical ranges: Shishu: 10-12% p.a., Kishore: 11-14% p.a., Tarun: 12-16% p.a. Women borrowers and SC/ST borrowers may get lower rates at some banks. Shop around and compare rates at 2-3 banks before deciding. Some state governments also offer interest subvention (subsidy) for Mudra borrowers.

faqs.question: Can I get Mudra loan for agriculture?
faqs.answer: Not for direct farming (crop cultivation), as agriculture has separate schemes like Kisan Credit Card. But you CAN get Mudra loan for allied agricultural activities: dairy farming, poultry, fisheries, beekeeping, mushroom cultivation, sericulture, food processing, and agro-processing. These are classified as micro-enterprises and are very common Mudra use cases.

faqs.question: What if I can't repay the Mudra loan?
faqs.answer: If you're facing genuine difficulty, contact your bank immediately and request restructuring or rescheduling of the loan. Banks can extend the repayment period or offer a moratorium. Do not default silently — it damages your CIBIL score and makes future borrowing impossible. Under RBI guidelines, banks must offer restructuring options before declaring a loan as NPA (Non-Performing Asset).

faqs.question: Can salaried people get Mudra loan?
faqs.answer: Mudra is specifically for self-employed individuals and micro-entrepreneurs, not for salaried employees. However, if you're salaried but running a business on the side, you can apply for Mudra for your business — but the loan must be used for business purposes only, not personal use.

faqs.question: Is there a Mudra loan subsidy?
faqs.answer: Mudra itself doesn't have a subsidy component — it's a loan that must be repaid with interest. However, some state governments offer interest subvention (partial interest subsidy) for Mudra borrowers. Check with your state's MSME department or the bank for any available interest subsidy schemes. Some states also offer subsidy specifically for women entrepreneurs or SC/ST borrowers.

faqs.question: What is Mudra Card?
faqs.answer: Mudra Card is a debit card linked to a working capital Mudra loan account. It works like a business credit card — you can withdraw or spend up to your sanctioned limit, and interest is charged only on the amount used. It's available for Shishu and Kishore categories and is particularly useful for businesses with fluctuating cash needs. The card provides convenience and better financial management.

faqs.question: How many times can I take a Mudra loan?
faqs.answer: There is no limit. You can take multiple Mudra loans over time, including upgrading from Shishu to Kishore to Tarun to Tarun Plus. However, you should have repaid or be regular on your previous Mudra loan before applying for a new one. Good repayment history increases your chances of higher loan amounts and better interest rates.
